steps.title: Pin down what needs supporting
steps.content: The scholars' claim is specific: the game was "largely played by traders." Strong support needs to tie the game itself — not just goods or geography in general — to traders as the primary users.

steps.title: Decide what counts as direct evidence
steps.content: Finding the game in places dominated by traders would directly support the claim. Finding it in a single trader-linked home, or finding unrelated trade goods nearby, would only loosely associate the game with trade, not show traders were the main players.

steps.title: Eliminate by overreach or misfit
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'in the remains of several homes... including in one home that may have belonged to a trader': most of the homes are unspecified; only one "may" have been a trader's. That points to mixed/household use, not predominantly trader use. ✗
  • Choice B — 'Similar games have been found in other sites... connected to the site in Oman via trade routes': shows the sites were linked by trade, but says nothing about who actually played the game there. ✗
  • Choice C — 'other known examples... found along routes that seem to have been used primarily by traders': places the game itself where traders were the dominant users, directly supporting that traders largely played it. ✓
  • Choice D — 'Remnants of other goods... probably also reached the location through trade': this is about other goods at the Oman site, not about who used the game. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
